title: Arrow vs Gladius
result: Even — pilot skill decides
detail: Arrow wins if it controls range and makes clean passes. Gladius wins if it absorbs the Arrow's burst and forces a sustained fight. Arrow's 75 pitch vs Gladius 68 is the agility edge but Gladius 6,336 HP is double the Arrow's 3,168 HP. The Gladius player should force nose-to-nose and trade hits — the Arrow cannot afford that exchange.
arrow_tips: Stay at 400-800m — do not close to sub-200m where Gladius shield advantage matters most
Fire missiles on the first pass to force evasion
Do not extend beyond 1.2km — your Deadbolts lose effectiveness at range
gladius_tips: Force knife-fight range — your dual engines keep you alive longer
Let the first Arrow pass hit your shield — 6,336 HP is enough to survive one burst
Counter-pass immediately — the Arrow cannot absorb return fire

title: Sabre vs Arrow
result: Sabre favoured — stealth approach is decisive
detail: If the Sabre player arrives cold (EM under scanner threshold), the Arrow never gets a radar lock before the opening burst lands. At 3,844 IR the Sabre is below IR missile tracking range for most seekers. The Arrow's 6,449 IR is comparatively loud. In a fair straight fight the Arrow's higher pitch (75 vs 62) gives it the agility edge — but the Sabre should never let it be a fair fight.
arrow_tips: Active scan before engaging — do not wait for passive detection
If caught by the Sabre's opener, use 229 SCM to disengage immediately
Never fight a Sabre in a sustained engagement — its 4× S3 simultaneous burst wins sustained DPS
gladius_tips: Arrive cold — stealth QD, full stealth component stack before engagement
Fire at sub-600m — inside reliable IR missile seeker range for Tempests
If the opener misses, disengage and reset the stealth approach

title: Ghost Mk II vs anything
result: Ghost wins the first engagement, loses if it fails
detail: IR 1,693 makes the Ghost Mk II genuinely invisible on passive scanners. At 6,083 EM idle with stealth components it sits below the threshold most players never scan past. The entire strategy is: arrive unseen, open at sub-600m, do not survive a sustained fight. 2× S4 Deadbolts hit hard but there are only two. Against anything with real shields (Shiv 7,200, Scorpius 10,560, Vanguard 21,120) the Ghost must land multiple passes which exposes it.
arrow_tips: Assume the Ghost is there before you see it — ping actively in contested space
If you take an unexpected burst from close range you are likely fighting a Ghost
Disengage immediately after taking a hit — do not turn to fight what you cannot see yet
gladius_tips: The Ghost player: commit to the first pass, disengage if target survives
Against heavies: multiple cold passes, never sustained — EM spikes on firing

title: Light fighters vs Scorpius / Shiv
result: Heavies favoured in sustained — lights win with swarm tactics
detail: A single Arrow or Gladius cannot out-DPS a Scorpius fast enough before dying. The Scorpius 10,560 HP shield absorbs 2-3 light fighter passes while returning fire from 7 hardpoints. The counter is multiple lights making simultaneous passes from different vectors — the Scorpius 42 pitch cannot track two threats at once. Two Arrows coordinated = force multiplier that the Scorpius cannot respond to.
arrow_tips: Solo light vs Scorpius: do not sustain — make passes and extend
Coordinated lights: split approaches — one from 12 o'clock, one from 6 o'clock simultaneously
Force the Scorpius to choose — it cannot track two passing targets at 42 pitch
gladius_tips: Scorpius pilots: keep the fight to one vector — do not let them split
Use 230 SCM to control engagement range — force lights to chase you into your guns
FR-76 M shield regen means you recover between each light fighter pass

title: Dealing with Banu Defender
result: Burst builds are countered — distortion or sustained wins
detail: The Banu Defender shield regenerating from 0% with no delay means standard alpha-burst tactics do not work the same way. A Sabre opening burst that fully depletes a human shield would then allow 4-5 seconds of no regen — against the Defender the regen starts immediately. The counter is either distortion weapons (bypass shield regen advantage) or sustained DPS that keeps the shield depleted faster than it regens.
arrow_tips: Do not open with everything at once — the shield will regen
Sustained Panther fire keeps shield depleted faster than Banu regen
Distortion weapons are the hard counter — distortion damage bypasses the regen mechanic
gladius_tips: Defender pilots: your shield is your advantage — let opponents commit their alpha, then counter
Tachyon cannons optimal at 600-1200m — force range, do not let lights close to sub-300m

title: Missiles — when to use
result: Missiles force evasion — use them to create openings, not to kill
detail: At current 4.8 point defence and flare mechanics, most pilots can avoid missiles if they are paying attention. The correct use is not "fire missiles to kill" but "fire missiles to force a defensive manoeuvre" — the target breaks off their attack run to evade, giving you a clean pass window. Fire missiles first, guns second. The few seconds the target is flaring/evading is when your Deadbolts should land.
arrow_tips: Fire IR Tempests as soon as you enter engagement range — the target must respond
Deadbolts should fire the moment missiles are away — the target cannot evade both simultaneously
S5 Ignite missiles on heavy targets: fire first from max seeker range, they cannot dodge a full S5 salvo
gladius_tips: On evasive PvP targets: missiles force a break — follow with guns into the break
Against NPC enemies at ERT: open with full salvo then guns — NPCs do not flare effectively
Do not hold missiles waiting for a perfect shot — a forced evasion is worth more than a hit

title: F8C Lightning vs Vanguard Harbinger
result: F8C favoured in agility — Vanguard wins the war of attrition
detail: The F8C has 6 pilot guns vs the Vanguard's S5 + 4× S2. The F8C's combined DPS is higher but the Vanguard 21,120 HP vs F8C 14,960 HP shield is a significant tank advantage. The F8C wins by out-manoeuvring and landing clean 6-gun convergence passes. The Vanguard wins by absorbing the F8C's alpha and returning fire from its superior shield pool. Key: at 210 SCM both are similar speed — neither can easily disengage. This fight goes to whoever lands hits more consistently.
arrow_tips: F8C: use pitch advantage to force high-aspect angles the Vanguard S2 wings cannot track
Open with full missile salvo to strip regen before sustained fight
6-gun convergence at 400-600m is your win condition — closer than that and you eat too much return fire
gladius_tips: Vanguard: absorb the opening burst, S5 nose shot is your primary damage
FR-76 M shield regen means you recover faster between passes than the F8C expects
Power triangle into shields during the F8C's burn — you outlast it if you avoid early spikes

title: Shiv vs Medium Fighters
result: Shiv favoured — cargo bed and M shield change the dynamic
detail: The Shiv at 7,200 HP M shield with 2× S4 pilot and S3 remote turret has more sustained capability than any solo medium fighter except the Vanguard. Against an F7C Mk II or Scorpius solo, the Shiv's cargo bed means a parked respawn point anywhere. The counter to the Shiv is flanking — its remote turret has coverage gaps at the nose. Getting inside 200m nose-to-nose where the turret cannot track is where mediums can compete. Against two mediums it becomes difficult — the M shield needs to absorb simultaneous passes.
arrow_tips: Medium fighters: force nose-to-nose engagement inside 200m — the remote turret has a dead zone there
Use higher pitch to reposition quickly — the Shiv 42 pitch is its limitation
Two coordinated mediums: split approach vectors, Shiv cannot track both directions simultaneously
gladius_tips: Shiv pilots: use the turret as a deterrent at 400-800m — make them commit to a vector
Bed is your respawn anchor — if killed, you come back local. Use this psychologically
Do not sustain nose-to-nose against high-pitch agile fighters — break off and reset at range

title: Fighting the Ares (Ion / Inferno)
result: Agile fighters hard-counter — stay mobile, never give a clean shot
detail: The Ares Ion and Inferno both mount a single fixed S7 gun. The S7 Laser Cannon has devastating energy alpha at range — a direct hit strips most fighter shields in one or two shots. The S7 Gatling (Inferno) has slower build-up but terrifying sustained DPS. Both are manually aimed fixed guns on a ship with 205 SCM and relatively poor agility. The counter is simple in theory: stay fast, change vector constantly, never give a clean angle. Lights and fast mediums hard-counter the Ares at close range. Large ships at range are its prey.
arrow_tips: Light fighters: approach from below 45 degrees — Ares pitch is limited, exploit it
Never fly straight for more than 2 seconds inside 2km of an Ares Ion
Close to sub-400m where the S7 Laser loses accuracy advantage — your guns are working, its is not
gladius_tips: Ares pilots: never fight agile fighters solo — you need escort
Your range advantage is everything — stay at 1-2km vs fighters, force them to close
Against large ships and ERTs you are the best ship in game — engage those, leave fighters to your escort

title: Vanguard vs Medium Wing (2v1)
result: 2× mediums favoured if coordinated — Vanguard wins if they engage sequentially
detail: A solo Vanguard Harbinger with FR-76 M shield and S5 Deadbolt can handle one medium indefinitely — the 21,120 HP shield absorbs what a single F7C or Scorpius puts out while returning fire. The problem for the Vanguard is two coordinated mediums attacking simultaneously. The 210 SCM is not fast enough to disengage from a pincer. The mediums win by splitting and forcing the Vanguard to choose one target while the other gets free shots from behind. The Vanguard wins if it engages one at a time — focus one medium, disengage before the second arrives.
arrow_tips: Two mediums: split approach at 180 degrees — Vanguard cannot turn its nose to both
One medium calls primary and draws fire, second medium attacks from the opposite vector
Do not both attack from the same bearing — that is the worst outcome, Vanguard can track you both
gladius_tips: Vanguard solo vs 2: pick the closer threat, commit to killing it before engaging the second
Power into shields and S5 gun — tunnel vision on one target until it dies or disengages
If both are outside 1km, use SCM to reposition — reset the engagement on your terms, not theirs

title: Scorpius vs F7C-M Super Hornet Mk II
result: Even at range — Super Hornet wins the knife fight
detail: The Scorpius has 4× S3 pilot guns + rear turret and 10,560 HP shield. The F7C-M Mk II has 2× S4 + S5 pilot + rear turret at 6,336 HP. The Scorpius out-sustains with more shield HP and 5-gun combined output from range. The Super Hornet wins inside 400m where its higher-size pilot guns (S4/S5) do more per shot and its rear turret with a gunner becomes devastating. Scorpius should fight at 600-1000m. Super Hornet should force a merge and stay merged.
arrow_tips: Super Hornet: force the merge — close fast, get inside 300m and make it a brawl
Rear gunner is your most valuable crew member in a knife fight — assign your best gunner
S4/S5 guns per shot > S3 — you win the trade inside close range where all guns bear
gladius_tips: Scorpius: stay at 600-1000m where your 4× S3 sustained DPS advantage holds
Do not let a Super Hornet inside 300m — it wins that range bracket
FR-76 M shield regen is your recovery tool — after each pass give the shield a beat before re-engaging

title: Counter-interdiction — escaping a Mantis snare
result: Target has options — react immediately or lose the initiative
detail: When a Mantis fires its quantum snare you have approximately 30 seconds of warning before it activates. The biggest mistake targets make is panicking and doing nothing. You have three options: burn toward the Mantis and destroy it before the snare fully charges (30 seconds is enough time in a fighter), QT to a random bearing immediately if the snare has not fully activated, or if snared — boost perpendicular to the Mantis and use mass dampener break technique (full boost + afterburner in a consistent direction breaks the dampener lock at approximately 2,200m/s delta-V). Freighters cannot break out — only fighters at high boost velocity.
arrow_tips: Snared target: burst in a single direction at maximum velocity — do not jink, do not change course
If you have a fighter: go kill the Mantis immediately — 30 seconds is a long time at fighter speed
Call your escorting fighters the moment you see a snare charging — their job is the Mantis, not the wing
gladius_tips: Mantis escort pilots: stay between 2-4km of the Mantis — close enough to react, far enough to see the fight develop
If the target turns on the Mantis: the escort engages immediately — Mantis is the priority asset
Do not charge snare until escort confirms the target is alone or pinned — a snare on an armed fighter with backup is suicide

title: Redeemer vs org fighters
result: Redeemer wins with crew — dies alone against coordinated lights
detail: A fully crewed Redeemer (2× S4 pilot + 4 turret guns firing) puts out combined DPS that rivals capital-class platforms. Against a single opponent it is effectively unkillable at its price point. Against a coordinated light fighter swarm the 40 pitch becomes fatal — two Arrows at 180° vectors means one always has a free shot on the Redeemer's belly or spine while it faces the other. Solo in the Redeemer: treat it like the Scorpius, use M-shield to absorb and S4 pilot DPS to finish. With crew: assign dedicated scanner to call out flankers before they get free angles.
arrow_tips: Lights vs Redeemer: never attack alone — two lights at opposite vectors simultaneously is the only reliable play
Target the pilot canopy — Redeemer pilot is not well-protected from forward aspect at knife range
If the Redeemer has a full crew, disengage — you cannot out-DPS coordinated turrets in a light fighter
gladius_tips: Redeemer pilots: M-shield is your safety net — power triangle into shields when taking coordinated hits
With full crew: pilot calls target and bearing, gunners suppress flankers. Do not let swarm split your attention
Solo vs lights: use SCM to create distance, use missile package to force evasion, avoid sustained nose-to-nose

title: F8C vs Asgard
result: F8C wins at range — Asgard wins at brawl range
detail: The F8C Lightning and Asgard represent opposite ends of the heavy fighter spectrum. The F8C has 210 SCM, 58 pitch, and 6 fast pilot guns — it controls range and lands clean passes. The Asgard has 32 pitch, 203 SCM, 21,120 HP shield, and 16× S3 missiles. The Asgard cannot chase the F8C and cannot dodge its passes. The F8C cannot survive the Asgard's missile opening or sustain against its superior shield pool in a prolonged fight. The F8C must land clean 6-gun convergence passes and disengage before the Asgard's missiles reach it. The Asgard must fire all 16 missiles before engagement and force the F8C into a brawl where the shield advantage dominates.
arrow_tips: F8C: open at max range — fire 8× S4 missiles first, then 6-gun convergence while Asgard is managing missile evasion
Never stay in a sustained nose-to-nose with an Asgard — 21,120 HP vs 14,960 HP, you lose attrition
Use 210 SCM to reset after each pass — the Asgard cannot follow at 32 pitch
gladius_tips: Asgard: fire all 16× S3 Ignites before the F8C closes to gun range — the salvo volume forces evasion
Once missiles are gone power 100% into shields and S5 gun — you win the sustained exchange
Do not try to chase — set your position and make the F8C come to you

title: Ares Ion — escort meta
result: Ion wins fleet fights with fighter cover — dies solo
detail: The Ares Ion S7 Laser is one of the most powerful single pilot guns in the game but it needs an escort to function. Solo the Ion is helpless against anything agile — a single Arrow closing to 400m ends the fight instantly. The correct usage: 1-2 fighters screening, Ion at 1-2km range picking capital and large targets. The fighters create space, the Ion strips shield HP from anything the fighters can't kill. In org fleet ops the Ion is the second deadliest asset after the Perseus. In solo or unescorted ops it is the most dangerous ship to fly.
arrow_tips: Ion pilots: always require 2 dedicated escorts before engaging — non-negotiable
Stay at 1,000-2,000m from the nearest fighter. Your S7 accuracy window is 600m-2km.
Call your escort callsigns on comms — they need to know where you are to protect you
gladius_tips: Escort pilots: position between the Ion and the nearest threat vector
Your job is not kills — it is keeping fighters away from the Ion
If an attacker gets past you to the Ion, immediately break off your target and intercept

title: Perseus fleet engagement
result: Perseus wins if it has fighter cover — loses to torpedo strike or boarding
detail: The Perseus 2× S7 turrets are devastating but they require gunners to aim manually. Against a coordinated attack the pilot is managing shields/power while gunners call and engage targets independently. The vulnerability window is the time between a threat appearing and a turret bearing on it — fast lights inside 400m can close before a turret rotates. Counter: torpedo strike from outside the S7 effective range (~2km), or interdiction + boarding while the shields are engaged. In fleet ops the Perseus should be the fire support anchor with fighters providing close-in screening.
arrow_tips: Attacking a Perseus: never approach from the front or rear — the S7 turrets have full coverage there
Approach from dorsal or ventral at high speed — S7 turrets have a slower rotation rate than fighters can fly
Coordinate torpedo strikes from 3km+ — outside effective S7 counter-fire range
gladius_tips: Perseus crew: assign sectors before combat — pilot on navigation and shield management, no pilot guns
Coordinator role calls priority threats and directs gunner attention — without coordination the turrets fight independently
If fighters break through your screen, power 100% shields and call for fighter support — do not attempt to fight light fighters with S7 turrets

title: Hammerhead — crew coordination
result: Full crew Hammerhead is near unkillable — undermanned it has fatal blind spots
detail: The Hammerhead's 6 S4 turrets cover every vector. With 7 crew the kill zone is a full sphere — anything within 2km is in crossfire from at least 2 turrets simultaneously. The breakdown happens with fewer crew. 4 crew leaves dorsal and ventral uncovered. Fighters that know this will always attack from above or below. The correct attack: identify turret coverage by observing where fire comes from, then approach the uncovered vector at full speed and knife-fight range. The correct defence: maintain full crew or keep an escort fighter covering the dorsal/ventral blind spots.
arrow_tips: Attacking undermanned Hammerhead: observe where the turret fire comes from to map coverage
Approach from uncovered dorsal or ventral vector — stay directly above or below the ship
Inside 400m the turrets cannot track — knife-fight range neutralises S4 turret advantage
gladius_tips: Hammerhead crew: always assign dorsal and ventral last — they are the hardest to cover solo but the most critical
Coordinator on comms calls incoming vector — gunners do not pick their own targets independently
If running with fewer than 6 crew, call for escort fighters on the uncovered sectors before entering contested space

title: Fighting the Polaris
result: Polaris wins at range — loses to knife range swarm or torpedo counter-strike
detail: The Polaris at range is almost unkillable. S10 torpedoes obliterate anything they hit and the 210 SCM means it can disengage faster than most fighters expect. The vulnerability is knife range — 12 pitch means the turrets cannot track agile fighters that get inside 500m. A coordinated swarm of 4+ lights attacking simultaneously from multiple vectors will overwhelm the turret coverage. The Polaris also cannot respond to its own torpedo counter — if the attacking force has torpedo-capable ships standing off at 5km+, the Polaris must either close range (losing its advantage) or absorb sustained torp fire. The low IR means the Polaris often arrives before it is detected — treat every contested zone as if a Polaris is already there.
arrow_tips: Attacking Polaris: never approach from any angle the turrets can track — come from above/below at maximum speed
Inside 400m the Polaris cannot fight effectively — get there and stay there
Coordinate torpedo or S5 missile strike from 5km+ while swarm keeps turrets occupied
gladius_tips: Polaris crew: engagement range is everything — stay at 3-8km and let torps do the work
Power down non-essential systems on approach — the low IR is your biggest tactical advantage
If fighters close to knife range: full power to shields, call for escort fighter support immediately

title: Polaris vs Hammerhead
result: Polaris wins at range with torpedo alpha — Hammerhead wins if it survives the opening salvo
detail: The Polaris S10 torpedo opening salvo is the deciding factor. A full 4-tube salvo against a Hammerhead will strip most of its L-shield in one volley. If the Hammerhead survives the opener — either through evasion, point defence, or the Polaris missing — the 6× S4 turrets become a sustained DPS advantage that the Polaris cannot match. The Polaris must fire first and hit. The Hammerhead must absorb the opener and close range to where the Polaris turrets cannot track. This is the org fleet matchup that defines capital engagements in 4.8.
arrow_tips: Polaris: fire full torpedo salvo at max range before the Hammerhead can close — do not hold for a better shot
After torpedo volley switch to turret fire immediately — do not let the Hammerhead have a free approach window
If the Hammerhead closes to under 1km, disengage at 210 SCM — you outrun it
gladius_tips: Hammerhead: use fighter screen to intercept torpedo salvos if point defence is available
Close range as fast as possible after the first torpedo volley — inside 1km the Polaris loses its main advantage
All 6 turret sectors on the Polaris at close range — the 12 pitch cannot respond to simultaneous angles

title: Polaris torpedo doctrine
result: Full salvo at range is the only correct use — partial salvos are wasted
detail: The S10 torpedo has a massive blast radius and each one carries enormous alpha damage. The doctrine is simple: never fire a partial salvo. Wait until the target is confirmed, fire all 4 tubes simultaneously, then reload. A staggered torp approach gives the target time to evade between shots. Simultaneous detonation from 4 torps in close proximity creates overlapping blast zones that are nearly impossible to evade even with a fast ship. The Polaris torpedo officer's job is timing — hold until the target's velocity vector is predictable, fire all 4, reload immediately. Against capital ships fire two salvos in sequence. Against fighter formations fire during a merge when evasion is limited.
arrow_tips: Polaris torpedo officer: never fire partial salvo — wait for the full 4-tube lock before committing
Optimal range: 3-6km — far enough for full torp speed, close enough for reliable tracking
Against fighters: fire during a head-on merge when evasion space is limited, not at retreating targets
gladius_tips: Defending against Polaris torps: break hard perpendicular the moment you see a launch signature — torps track slow manoeuvrers
If you have point defence: activate the moment you see torp separation from the Polaris tubes
Never fly straight within 6km of a known Polaris position — the torp flight time is short enough to catch you

title: Polaris vs Perseus
result: Polaris wins at range — Perseus wins in sustained close engagement
detail: Both are capital-class ships but they have fundamentally different strengths. The Polaris S10 torpedoes do more damage per volley than the Perseus S7 turrets can output in the same timeframe. The Perseus L-shield at 211,200 HP can absorb a partial Polaris salvo — but a full simultaneous 4-tube volley will strip it entirely. The Perseus counter: close range quickly using fighter screen cover, deny the Polaris the 3-6km window it needs for effective torpedo fire. Inside 1km the Polaris 12 pitch cannot track the Perseus at all and the S7 turrets become dominant. The Perseus wins the sustained fight; the Polaris wins the opening engagement.
arrow_tips: Polaris: fire full torpedo salvo before the Perseus closes to under 2km — this is your only window
After torpedo volley the fight is effectively decided — if the Perseus absorbed it full power to shields and disengage
Never let the Perseus close to under 1km — the 12 pitch cannot respond at that range
gladius_tips: Perseus: use every fighter escort to intercept torpedo salvos on approach
Close range aggressively — every meter inside 2km reduces the Polaris torpedo effectiveness
Once inside 1km: both S7 turrets on the Polaris simultaneously — it cannot dodge and cannot track

title: Hammerhead vs Starlancer TAC
result: Hammerhead wins at medium range — Starlancer wins sustained close brawl
detail: The Hammerhead 6× S4 full-sphere turrets vs the Starlancer TAC 144,000 HP L-shield and 4× S4 pilot guns. At 600-1500m the Hammerhead's combined 6× S4 DPS is higher than the Starlancer can put out with pilot guns alone. But the Starlancer's L-shield absorbs sustained S4 fire far longer than the Hammerhead's L-shield can hold under the same pressure — because the Starlancer also has 4× S4 pilot plus turrets returning fire simultaneously. The critical variable: crew count. A fully crewed Hammerhead (7 crew) wins. An understaffed Hammerhead (3-4 crew) with uncovered dorsal/ventral sectors loses badly — the Starlancer pilot can exploit those blind spots with 4× S4 pilot guns from an angle the turrets can never reach.
arrow_tips: Hammerhead vs Starlancer: maintain 600-1000m range where 6× S4 simultaneous fire advantage matters
Assign all 6 turrets before the fight — specifically fill dorsal and ventral to deny the Starlancer blind spot access
If the Starlancer closes to under 300m: power 100% shields and call for fighter screen — you cannot track at knife range
gladius_tips: Starlancer TAC: close range aggressively — your L-shield absorbs the Hammerhead DPS while you approach
Once inside 200m exploit any uncovered dorsal/ventral sector with 4× S4 pilot guns — the turrets cannot follow
With full escort the fight is yours — coordinate with fighter screen to force the Hammerhead to split fire between you and them
